coredata.dc:title: DFT investigation of graphene quantum dot-Ixora floral natural dye (GQD-NDIX) nanocomposites as visible light harvesters in dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wazzan, Nuha
Irfan, Ahmad
Fagieh, Taghreed M.
coredata.dc:description: As reported, the power conversion efficiencies of photosensitized solar cells with the four natural dyes from Ixora floral (NDIXs) were less than 1%, resulting from poor absorption of light in the visible (Vis) and near infrared (NIR) regions. The four NDIXs are aurone (ND1), pelargonidin (ND2), cyanidin (ND3) and malvidin (ND4). In this work, the study was conducted to investigate the effect of hybridizing the four NDIXs with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The four NDIXs were studied computationally at the DFT/TDDFT with B3LYP/CAM-B3LYP functionals and 6-31G(d,p)/LANL2DZ basis sets all with the C-PCM solvation model. All the calculated parameters preferred the GQD-NDIX nanocomposites (NCs). NCs have appropriate HOMO and LUMO energy levels, absorbed light in the Vis region with a much higher light harvesting efficiency (by a difference not less than 5%), and significantly improve the intramolecular charge transfer (IACT) property necessary for efficient electron injection. Among the three configurations of the possible adsorption systems of NCs on TiO2, the NCs adsorbed on TiO2 through a covalent interaction between the NDs and TiO2 and not by the GQD adsorbed on TiO2 were evident by comparing the adsorption energies of different adsorption systems (the difference is not less than 0.7 eV). The optical properties of isolated dyes/nanocomposites were compared and discussed, and the optical properties of TiO2 adsorbed systems were calculated and discussed. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties were compared and proved to be significantly enhanced and thus the IACT abilities in the adsorbed systems. This work can be used as a useful source for developing GQD-NDIX photosensitizers as efficient visible light harvesters in DSSC applications.

head.ce:title: DFT investigation of graphene quantum dot-Ixora floral natural dye (GQD-NDIX) nanocomposites as visible light harvesters in dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As reported, the power conversion efficiencies of photosensitized solar cells with the four natural dyes from Ixora floral (NDIXs) were less than 1%, resulting from poor absorption of light in the visible (Vis) and near infrared (NIR) regions. The four NDIXs are aurone (ND1), pelargonidin (ND2), cyanidin (ND3) and malvidin (ND4). In this work, the study was conducted to investigate the effect of hybridizing the four NDIXs with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The four NDIXs were studied computationally at the DFT/TDDFT with B3LYP/CAM-B3LYP functionals and 6-31G(d,p)/LANL2DZ basis sets all with the C-PCM solvation model. All the calculated parameters preferred the GQD-NDIX nanocomposites (NCs). NCs have appropriate HOMO and LUMO energy levels, absorbed light in the Vis region with a much higher light harvesting efficiency (by a difference not less than 5%), and significantly improve the intramolecular charge transfer (IACT) property necessary for efficient electron injection. Among the three configurations of the possible adsorption systems of NCs on TiO2, the NCs adsorbed on TiO2 through a covalent interaction between the NDs and TiO2 and not by the GQD adsorbed on TiO2 were evident by comparing the adsorption energies of different adsorption systems (the difference is not less than 0.7 eV). The optical properties of isolated dyes/nanocomposites were compared and discussed, and the optical properties of TiO2 adsorbed systems were calculated and discussed. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties were compared and proved to be significantly enhanced and thus the IACT abilities in the adsorbed systems. This work can be used as a useful source for developing GQD-NDIX photosensitizers as efficient visible light harvesters in DSSC applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural dyes (NDs) are (1) readily available, (2) environmentally friendly, (3) easy to extract from their native planets and sources, and (4) usually extracted with high purity. The application of naturally occurring dyes/pigments extracted from avocado, pomegranates, berry fruits, Reseda luteola, Berberisintegerrima, Panica granatum Consolida orientalis, Pleniflora, Clemetis orientalis, Reseda gredensis, Adonisflammea, Salvia sclarea, Consolidaajacis, Saffron, red onion, Mallow, Origanum vulgare, Acanthus Euphorbia cotinifolia leaf and Sennii chiovenda flower [1–5] as potential photosensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) applications has stimulated worldwide concern.

Most of the sunlight that strikes the Earth’s atmosphere is in the visible (Vis) and near infrared (NIR) regions, and it is essential that the photosensitizer (the component responsible for light absorption) can absorb light strongly in these regions. However, the recorded lower conversion efficiencies of DSSCs with NDs as photosensitizers referred to their weak absorption of light in the Vis and NIR regions, which led to lower photoelectrical conversion power [6] and to the barrier of electron transfer in the TiO2 semiconductor layer [7]. The applied strategies to improve the optical absorption properties of NDs include cosensitization procedures, i.e., the use of natural dye mixtures [8], the use of different extraction techniques, modification of the pH (acidity) of the solvent used for the extraction process, and the use of a variety of solvent combinations [9]. Another effective strategy is the hybridization of the ND with a quantum dot (QD) material [6,10–13]. The hybridization of ND with a QD material to form a nanocomposite is an efficient tool to enhance the photoelectric conversion power because the photovoltaic performance of nanocomposite materials is better than that of isolated components [6].

Quantum dots (QDs), first characterized in 1983 [14], are semiconductor nanoparticles with diameters in the nanometer (nm) range. The “quantum confinement” in QDs gives rise to exceptional optical and electronic properties, very different than those of a related bulk material. Additionally, due to the small dimensions of QDs, the energy levels are distinct; therefore, it is possible to control the energy gap between the valence and conductance bands during the synthesis of nanocrystals. At present, QDs are well known because they absorb photons in the whole spectrum and radiate narrow light. QDs can be made from single-element materials, such as graphene (G) [15,16], silicon [17] or germanium [18], or from compound semiconductors, such as CdSe [19] and CdTe [20]. One of the most important QDs is graphene QDs (GQDs) because of their good mechanical properties and large charge mobility, in addition to their optical and electrical properties. These properties qualify GQDs to be excellent candidates to form nanocomposites with enhanced photovoltaic properties [21].

Here, we designed four nanocomposites, each of which comprises GQDs (C80H26) hybridized with one of the four NDs extracted from IX flowers [25]. We performed DFT and time-dependent functional (TDDFT) calculations to obtain the optical and electronic properties of the GQD-NDIX nanocomposites. Moreover, the interaction and charge transfer mobility between GQD-NDIX nanocomposites and TiO2 (represented successfully by (Ti2O4H)3 [26,27]) semiconductors are investigated. The assumption that the electronic and optical properties of GQD-NDIX are better than those of a single NDIX component will be investigated and interpreted.

Most of the density functionals were incapable of properly describing the van der Waals interactions due to dynamical correlations between fluctuating charge distributions. A practical method to solve this issue was achieved by the DFT-D approach, which added a semiempirical dispersion potential to the conventional Kohn–Sham DFT energy [37]. The dye moiety is attached to the GQD, and the GQD moiety is attached to TiO2 through a single C−C linkage. The interaction between the three species would be better understood by inclusion of dispersion correction. The B3LYP functional is associated with the dispersion correction term “D” of Grimme [29,38,39] using Gaussian semiempirical dispersion = gd3 keyword.

Based on the optimized geometrical structures, we calculate the absorption spectra, vertical excitation energies, oscillator strengths, and light harvesting efficiency (LHE) by applying the TDDFT. We choose the hybrid exchange-correlation functional (CAM–B3LYP) [40] for these calculations since the CAM-B3LYP functional provides a good description of the charge-transfer excitations [41].

We performed vibrational frequency calculations at the same level of theory to ensure that all optimized free structures and complex systems correspond to local minima on their potential energy surface, and analysis of the frequencies revealed no imaginary (negative) frequencies in any case.

The vertical excitation energies for the fifty lowest spin-allowed singlet transitions are calculated for the isolated NDs, nanocomposites and adsorbed system using TDDFT/CAM-B3LYP with the C-PCM in methanol. The detailed equations and literature used to calculate different parameters can be found in the Supplementary data (DFT details).

Noncovalent interactions (NCIs) were performed by the Multiwfn 3.7 software package [42] to understand the nature of interactions between various fragments of the system. The isosurfaces of the GQD-ND nanocomposite were made using visual molecular dynamics (VMD) 1.9.3 [43]. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps were drawn using the Avogadro package [44]. The calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 suite program [45], and the structures were visualized using the Gaussview program (version 5.0.8) [46] and Chemcraft program version 1.8 (build 489) [47].

The total and partial (T/PDOS) values ​​are calculated for ND1-ND4 to shed light on the structure-property relationship, see Fig. 2 . The results after analysis show that they have improved s-orbital contribution in low energy levels. The peaks of the orbitals (-s) are observed between −0.8 and −1.0 eV in valence bands (VB), with little contribution in the conduction bands (CB). The most important peaks in low VB in the sequence of ND3 > ND4 > ND2 > ND1 are due to the s-orbital contributions to these compounds, respectively. Where p-orbitals dominate PDOS and TDOS between 0 to −1.0 eV but mainly from 0 to −0.5 eV in VB. Minor participation of p-orbitals can also be found in CB from 0 to 0.2 eV. The large VB peaks near the fermi level are caused by p-orbital contributions to these compounds, respectively. The DOS diagram shows the energy bands next to the Fermi bands arranged in p-orbitals that illustrate the contribution of p-orbitals in optoelectronic structures. It shows the significant role of p-orbitals in the energy states near the Fermi level. TDOS and PDOS for ND1-ND4 show participation of s/p-orbitals in electronic properties. It shows that these compounds with good optoelectronic properties would be handsome competitors for photovoltaic applications. In addition, structural studies have shown that these compounds with fascinating properties of interests can lead to the use of semiconductors device.

Fig. 3 shows the optimized geometries of GQD-NDIX nanocomposites at the C-PCM/B3LYP-D/6-31G(d,p) corrected with the CB/BSSE method in methanol solvent. The absence of any imaginary frequencies indicates that the optimized geometries correspond to minima points in their potential energy surface. The numbers in parentheses in Fig. 3 show the frequency range of each optimized nanocomposite geometry. In addition, Fig. 4 shows the IR spectra of the four investigated nanocomposites.

NCI analysis provides an index based on the electron density and its derivatives that enables the identification of noncovalent interactions, as developed by Johnson et al. [48]. It is based on a 2D plot of the reduced density gradient, RDG, and the electron density,
ρ
, according to the following equation:
(1)
RDG =

ρ
r
is the mode of the electron density gradient, and
ρ
r
is the electronic density. We attempted to examine the interaction between the GQDs and NDs by investigating the intermolecular interaction by noncovalent interaction-reduced density gradient (NCI-RDG) plot analysis. NCI-RDG analysis can provide details regarding weak NCI in real space and visualize different regions where NCI appears. Therefore, through NCI, a clear idea regarding the various types of interactions involved in the nanocomposites, such as hydrogen bonding (HB), van der Waals (vdW), and steric clashes (SCs), can be deduced. NCI-RDG 2D scatter maps of GQD-NDIX nanocomposites at C-PCM/B3LYP-D/6-31G(d,p) in methanol solvent are represented in Fig. 5 and Figure SD1 (noncolored NCI-RDG 2D scatter maps).

Attractive and binding interactions (strong hydrogen bond interaction) if
λ < 0
and repulsive and nonbinding interactions (steric effect in ring and cage) if
λ > 0
, where van der Waals interactions are indicated by
λ
≈ 0
, refer to the top illustration plot in Fig. 5. In the RDG plot, three colored regions represent three types of interactions. The green region represents the van der Waals interactions, the repulsive interactions due to strong steric effect are represented by red colored region, while the blue region referred to strong attraction due to hydrogen bond (H-bond), refer to the top illustration plot in Fig. 5. NCI results reveal that the three types of NCIs are present in the GQD-ND nanocomposites. The three GQD-ND1/3/4 nanocomposites show plates with similar positions and intensities, especially GQD-ND3/4. However, GQD-ND2 shows spikes somehow different than those of the other nanocomposites (more condensed red plates). Since GQD-ND2 is the only one among the four nanocomposites optimized geometries shows a parallel orientation between the two fragments (ND and GQD), this result in elevation of the steric clashes between the aromatic rings of the two fragments. Additionally, this nanocomposite shows the most condensed green spikes, indicating more obvious vdW interactions [49]. Additionally, GQD-ND1 shows the least NCI resulting from the H-bond interaction indicated by the weak appearance of blue spikes.

One of the top requirements that determines the suitability of the photosensitizer as an active component in the fabricated DCCS is the proper level alignment between its HOMO and LUMO energy levels with those of the conduction band (CB) of the TiO2 semiconductor (
- 3.74
eV) and the redox potential (RP) of the
I
-
/
I
3
-
electrolyte (
- 4.8
eV). Where the LUMOs of the photosensitizers should be situated above the CB level of TiO2, the HOMOs should be situated below the RP level of the
I
-
/
I
3
-
electrolyte. Proper energy level alignment will ease the most important processes that occur in the DCCS framework, i.e., electron injection and sensitizer regeneration.

Fig. 6 shows the energy levels of HOMOs and LUMOs the nanocomposites match with CB of the TiO2 electrode and the RP of
I
-
/
I
3
-
electrolyte calculated at C-PCM/CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) in methanol solvent. From Fig. 6, it is evident that the LUMOs are situated above the CB by not less than 0.469 eV. In addition, the HOMOs are situated below the RP by not more than 0.389 eV. Therefore, the nanocomposites of GQD-NDIX as components have the proper HOMOs and LUMOs energy levels to transfer the charges (electrons and holes) with the other components in the DCCS.

From Table SD1, it is evident that the combination of NDIX and GQDs results in significant elevation and lowering of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels, respectively. The HOMO energy levels of the isolated NDIX fall in a small range from −7.264 to −7.440 eV, while those of the GQD-ND nanocomposites range from −4.795 to −5.189 eV. The LUMOs of isolated dyes are in the range of −1.324 to −2.398 eV, and for the nanocomposites, the range is from −3.113 to −3.266 eV. The HOMO levels of the nanocomposite are much closer to the RP of the electrolyte, which is beneficial for the thermodynamic spontaneity of the hole flow from these nanocomposites to the RP of the electrolyte. Similarly, the LUMOs of the nanocomposites are closer to TiO2′s CB, which will help in easing—to a great extent—the electron injection process.

As the energy gap (
E
g a p
) decreases, the electron can be excited from the ground state to the excited state more easily, which would result in a greater capability to harvest sunlight [23]. The
E
g a p
values of the isolated NDIX are much larger than those of the nanocomposite, which clearly demonstrates the effect of GQDs in lowering the
E
g a p
values and subsequently boosting the electron excitation and enhancing the light absorption (see Table SD1). The
E
g a p
values of the isolated NDIX are in the range from 4.866 eV to 6.084 eV, while for the nanocomposites, it is in the range from 1.682 eV to 1.923 eV, closely related to that of the isolated GQD (1.660 eV). The order in which the
E
g a p
values of the nanocomposites decrease is ND4-GQD (1.923 eV)
≤
ND3-GQD (1.921 eV) < ND2-GQD (1.801 eV) < ND1-GQD (1.682 eV). Therefore, by using GQD-NDIX nanocomposites, the LUMOs, HOMOs and their gaps can be efficiently tuned, and the sensitizer regeneration, electron injection, and charge excitation performances can be improved [24].

Chemical reactivity parameters such as (i) electron affinity (
EA
), (ii) electrophilicity index (
ω
), (iii) electroaccepting power (
ω
+
), and (iv) chemical hardness (
h
), which are listed in Table 2 , are good indices used to evaluate the photoelectric performance of different sensitizers. A high value of
EA
is beneficial for electron transportation.
ω
is the measure of the stabilization energy of the molecular system. A higher
ω
value means that molecules have a better molecular electron-donating capacity. The highest
ω
+
represents the highest electron-accepting ability. Thus, higher
ω
and
ω
+
are preferred too. The chemical hardness of a molecule in its ground state is its resistance to exchange electronic charge with its environment. The
η
value can be considered a measure of the ability of the dye to transfer charge, as it decreases as this ability increases and vice versa [50]. Therefore, as the
EA
,
ω
, and
ω
+
values become larger, the charge transfer performance improves and the short-circuit current (
J
sc
)
increases. To obtain the same properties, smaller

Comparing the
EA
values of the isolated NDIX and those of the nanocomposites reveals that the values of the isolated dyes are significantly smaller, indicating that the electron transfer ability is significantly improved in the nanocomposites. The order of increasing the
EA
values for NDIX is ND1 (1.32 eV) < ND2 (2.36 eV) < ND3 (2.38 eV) < ND4 (2.40 eV), and the order shown by the nanocomposites is ND1-GQD (3.113 eV) < ND3-GQD (3.265 eV)
≤
ND4-GQD (3.266 eV) < ND2-GQD (3.271 eV). The
ω
and
ω
+
values for the nanocomposites become larger (less negative and more positive, respectively) than those of the isolated ND. Therefore, the electron donating/accepting abilities of the nanocomposites are better than those of isolated ND, which is beneficial for better performance of the sensitizer with the DSSC framework. On the other hand, the NDIX hardness values are in the range from 2.43 to 3.04 eV, considerably higher than those of the nanocomposites (range from 0.84 to 0.96 eV). Keep in mind the effect of interaction between the GQDs and NDIX results in different effects on the abovementioned values, i.e., the order of increasing these values for the nanocomposites is different from that of the isolated NDIX. In conclusion, it is expected that the charge transfer performance and
J
sc
values for the nanocomposites are better than those of their isolated NDIX, and the ND2-GQD nanocomposite has the most efficient
EA
,
ω
,
ω
+
, and
h
values.

From Fig. 7, the effect of solvent (methanol) as a mimic using the C-PCM model of solvation is obvious. The isolated NDIX and GQD-NDIX nanocomposites absorbed light more efficiently in methanol than the intensities of the absorption bands in the gas phase. Since the experimental data were carried out in methanol [25], Table 3 gives the absorption characteristics in methanol solvent. Upon photoexcitation, the isolated NDIX dyes showed two bands, one in the UV region (175–185 nm) and the other in the Vis region (348–497 nm). On the other hand, upon photoexcitation, the nanocomposites showed no absorption in the UV region; instead, two strong bands (overlapping in the simulated spectra) with much higher intensities appeared in the Vis region (410–442 nm). Without doubt, the facilitated broad spectral response in the Vis region with much higher intensities due to the incorporation of GQD-NDIX nanocomposites is expected to be very valuable to enhance the efficiency of DSSCs [23]. The light harvesting efficiency (LHE), is very closely related to the oscillator strength parameters. The
f
values increase tremendously due to the nanocomposites. The
f
values for NDIX are in the range of 0.4881 to 1.0400, while for the nanocomposites, the range is 0.9410 to 4.8232. Accordingly, in most cases, the LHEs for the nanocomposites reached unity, while for the isolated NDs, the maximum value was 0.909 (see Table 3). In addition, the composition of the transition of the most intense bands in the isolated nanocomposites and nanocomposites are not the same, since in NDIX, they are mainly due to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) transitions, while for the nanocomposites, molecular orbitals other than HOMOs and LUMOs are involved, such as HOMO-2 (H-2) and LUMO-6 (L-6), see Table 3.

Since the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) show the distribution of electrons and holes of HOMO and LUMO, and based on the absorption spectra data, the important FMOs that are expected to be involved in the photoexcitation process for ND1, ND3, GQD-ND1, and GQD-ND3 as representative examples are plotted in Fig. 8 , similar data for other systems are plotted in Figure SD2. Fig. 8 shows that the separation of charges, i.e., the HOMO and LUMO distributed on different parts of molecules, is not obvious in the isolated NDIX molecules, while the HOMOs and LUMOs are distributed differently on the nanocomposite molecules.

The emission properties of isolated NDs and the nanocomposites were calculated and are listed in Table SD2. The emission bands of NDs and nanocomposites are blueshifted (shorter wavelengths) compared to their absorption bands, except for ND1, where its emission bands (

The extent of IACT can be quantified using Le Bahers’s special indices of charge-transfer excitations between the ground and excited states (here, it is the 1st excited state), such as the charge transfer distance (

CT
)
; details on the definition and equations of these parameters can be found in [54]. Generally, larger values of
D
CT
,
q
CT
, and
μ
CT
and smaller values of
H
, and
t
, indicate an enhancement in the IACT [55]. Le Bahers’s special indices of isolated NDIX and nanocomposites of GQD-NDIX in methanol at C-PCM/TDCAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) are collected in Table 4 . From Table 4, the nanocomposites have better special indices than those of their isolated dyes; thus, it is predicted that the nanocomposites would have better IACT properties.

The values of
D
CT
for the isolated dyes do not exceed 1.910 Å in the ND2 dye, while those for the nanocomposites reach 4.490 Å in the ND4-GQD nanocomposite. The
μ
CT
values of the nanocomposites are generally significantly larger/larger than those of isolated dyes, indicating that during the IACT process, the positive and negative density regions are delocalized in different moieties of the molecules. Thus, the reorganization of charge density results in an increase in the molecular dipole moment [55,56]. As reported [57], if the
t
index values are larger than 1.6 Å, the functional used is adequate in describing the transition energy. All the obtained values are larger than 1.6 and in the range from −1.645 to −4.422 Å. Additionally, all the
t
-index values are much less than zero, indicating that the excitation can be attributed to the local excitation type [58]. The
t
values for the nanocomposite are better (=larger) than those of the isolated dyes. If the value of
t
is larger, it indicates that the overlap of orbitals of electrodonating and electroaccepting regions is small. The values of
t
suggest that the nanocomposites can effectively assist charge separation in the IACT process, resulting in favorable electron injection [23]. However, the
q
CT
values for the isolated dyes are generally larger than those of the nanocomposites, with the exception of ND3 (0.850 e) and ND3-GQD (0.915 e). The
H
values of the nanocomposites can be arranged in the following order: ND4-GQD (7.387 Å) > ND3-GQD (6.897 Å) > ND2-GQD (6.886 Å) > ND1-GQD (6.318 Å), which will give the order of decreasing the distance of the centroid axis along the charge transfer direction. Only ND3-GQD shows a larger
q
CT
value (0.915 e) than its isolate ND3 dye (0.850 e). In summary, the nanocomposites can efficiently enhance the IACT properties.

Electrons will be injected from the LUMO level of the excited photosensitizer to the CB of the TiO2 semiconductor, and the electron injection rate can be expressed by the free energy of electron injection (
Δ G
i n j
), Equation 3 (DFT details SD). A negative
Δ G
i n j
value means that a dye has a sufficient driving force to ensure the smooth injection of electrons into TiO2. As shown in Fig. 10 and Table SD4, some of the NDIX dyes have positive
Δ G
i n j
values, such as ND2 (
0.853
eV) and ND4 (0.769 eV). In comparison, the nanocomposite has a negative or a much more negative
Δ G
i n j
value. The
Δ G
i n j
values of the nanocomposite are in the range from
-
1.682 to
-
2.009 eV, inferring that the nanocomposites of GQD-NDIX will considerably increase the driving force of the electron injection process [23,27,59,60].

The photosensitizer regeneration process comes after the electron injection process since the oxidized photosensitizer needs to return to its unoxidized state by drawing electrons and donating holes from and to the electrolyte, respectively. This mutual process is important for the continuity and stability of DSSCs. Similarly, to achieve efficient regeneration, sufficient free energy for dye regeneration (
Δ G
r e g
), Equation 5 (DFT details SD), is required. For the GQD-NDIX nanocomposites, the
Δ G
r e g
values are small or negative. The order of increasing the
Δ G
r e g
values is ND1-GQD (−0.005 eV) < ND2-GQD (0.272 eV) < ND3-GQD (0.387 eV) < ND4-GQD (0.389 eV); see Fig. 10 and Table SD4. For isolated NDIX, they are in the range from 2.464 eV to 2.640; therefore, the
Δ G
r e g
values of the nanocomposites are much smaller than those of the isolated natural dyes, indicating that hybrid GQD-NDIX nanocomposites can considerably improve the process of photosensitizer regeneration [23]. Therefore, the designed GQD-NDIX nanocomposites are promising candidates to improve the photovoltaic performance of DSSCs.

The open-circuit voltage (
V
o c
), see Equation (1) (DFT details SD), of the solar cell is defined as the voltage that compensates the current flow through the external circuit [61], see Table SD4. The values of
V
o c
for the nanocomposites are smaller than those of the isolated NDIX. However, the order in which their
V
o c
values increase is ND1-GQD (0.495 eV) < ND2-GQD (0.772 eV) < ND3-GQD (0.887 eV)
≤
ND4-GQD (0.889 eV).

A direct relationship exists between the performance of a sensitizer and its nonlinear optical (NLO) properties. Since the NLO and photovoltaic properties are determined by the delocalization of intramolecular charges [23,62], both properties are needed to understand the electron transfer efficiency. Linear correlations were found between the two properties since the NLO properties could be used to describe the power conversion efficiency of electron charge transfer of the sensitizers, which could influence the
J
s c
and
η
values [62]. NLO properties can be quantitively measured by parameters such as the static dipole moment (
μ
), first-order hyperpolarizability
(
β 0
), and mean polarizability (
α 0
). The NLO parameters of the isolated NDIX and GQD-NDIX calculated at C-PCM/B3LYB and C-PCM/CAM-B3LYP with a 6-31G(d,p) basis set in methanol solvent are listed in Table 5 The used equations are detailed in refs. [63,64].

The static diploe moments, first-order hyperpolarizabilities, and mean polarizabilities of the nanocomposite materials are greater/much greater than those of their components (isolated dyes), which indicates the better NLO properties of the nanocomposites. Therefore, these nanocomposites will show a higher
η
than isolated NDs. The minimum value
μ
of the NDs is 3.87 Debye and the maximum value equals 12.45 Debye using the two methods (B3LYP and CAM-B3LYP). The minimum value of
μ
for the nanocomposites is 4.52 Debye and the maximum value equals 29.89 Debye. The minimum and maximum
β 0
values for the isolated NDs are
4 ×
10
- 29
esu and
16 ×
10
- 29
esu, respectively. For the nanocomposites, their minimum and maximum values are
100 ×
10
- 29
and
1227 ×
10
- 29
esu. For,
α 0
, the minimum values are
347 ×
10
- 29
esu and
2679 ×
10
- 29
for the NDs and nanocomposites, respectively. For the NDs and nanocomposites, their maximum values are
559 ×
10
- 29
esu and
5193 ×
10
- 29
, respectively. The trend observed by the two methods is not consistent; however, it was evident that the NLO parameters obtained using long-range exchange functionals such as CAM-B3LYP are more reliable than those obtained using short-range exchange functionals such as B3LYP [65,66]. Additionally, it was evident that the B3LYP usually overestimated the
β 0
values, as observed here since most of the
β 0
values obtained by the B3LYP method are higher than those obtained with the CAM-B3LYP method. Therefore, we will rank the NLO properties of the nanocomposites according to the obtained values at the CAM-B3LYP method. The order in which the
β 0
and
α 0
values increase for the nanocomposite materials is ND4-GQD < ND3-GQD < ND2-GQD < ND1-GQD. The
μ
values increase in a slightly different order, i.e., ND1-GQD < ND3-GQD < ND2-GQD < ND2-GQD. The
β
xxx
components exhibited the maximum values among all other
β 0
components, indicating that electrons will transfer mostly in one direction.

For the resultant systems (GQD-NDIX-I2), the basis set superposition error (BSSE) was estimated using the counterpoise (CP) method [67]. BSSE typically originated due to the incompleteness description of the basis set in the calculations of weak intermolecular interactions [68]. The BSSE corrected binding energies (
E
b i n d
B S S E
) of all optimized systems were calculated using the following equation:
(2)
E
b i n d
B S S E
= E
( N D I X - G Q D -
I 2
)
-
E
N D I X - G Q D
+ E
I 2
+
E
B S S E
)

where
E ( N D I X - G Q D -
I 2
) is the energy term of the complex system due to the interaction between the NDIX-GQD nanocomposite and I2 molecule,
E
N D I X - G Q D
and
E
I 2
are the energy terms of the two free components of the system, and
E
B S S E
corresponds to the BSSE energy.

During the photoexcitation process, the excited electron that forms an excited dye and leaves a hole can follow one of the three competing routes: (i) it can be effectively injected into the CB of TiO2, (ii) it can recombine with the hole in the dye to form the unexcited dye molecule, and (iii) it can recombine with the RP of the
I
-
/
I
3
-
electrolyte. The first route is known as electron injection, and it is a favorable route that results in electron photovoltage gain and higher
V
o c
. While the latter two routes are known as charge recombination, they are unfavorable routes because they result in undesired effects [60,69,70]. In this work, we aim to investigate the intermolecular interaction between the GQD-NDIX nanocopmosites and the electrolyte. Since the senstizer can react with the redox couple (iodine/iodide), it was confirmed that the intermolecular interaction with iodine (
I 2
) is doubled faster than that with iodide [60,71]; hence, the interaction with
I 2
is only considered in this work. If the intermolecular interaction with
I 2
is associated with a higher binding energy or if there are many active sites on the senstizers to react with
I 2
, the electrolyte concentration at the surface of the senstizers leads to isolation and effective injection of the electrtons to TiO2. The high electrolyte concentration shortens the lifetime of the electrons in TiO2 and accelerates charge recombination [69,70].

To obtain the active site on each GQD-NDIX (electron-rich region = nucleophilic attack region), the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) is used to predict the nucleophilic and electrophilic attack regions (see Fig. 11 ). The different colored regions qualitatively determine the region rich/poor in electrons. The blue-colored region indicates a reactive region for nucleophilic attack, while the red-colored region indicates a reactive region for an electrophilic attack, and the color changes as red–orange-white–blue. From Fig. 11, the amide linkage is the most reddish region on each GQD-NDIX framework, the amide linkage was selected to interact with the
I 2
molecule, and electrostatic attraction will occur between the electron-rich region in the sensitizer and the molecular iodine.

On the other hand, and as a representative example, only GQD-ND1-I2 was optimized. The optimized geometry of the GQD-ND1-I2 complex at C-PCM/B3LYP-D with a 6-31G(d,p) basis set for all atoms and LANL2DZ for iodine atoms corrected with the CB/BSSE method in methanol solvent along with its binding energy components is presented in Table SD5.

The binding energies of the GQD-ND1-I2 complex with the BSSE correction (
E
b i n d
w i t h
B S S E
)
and without it
E
b i n d
w i t h o u t
B S S E
) are
-
0.350 and
-
0.372 eV, respectively. Therefore, the % deviation is insignificant and does not exceed 6%, which indicates the correctness of the obtained binding energy without the BSSE correction. The distance between the N-amide linkage and iodine atom of the
I 2
molecule is 4.369 Å. On the other hand, comparison between the binding energy of the GQD-ND1-I2 complex and the adsorption energies of NDIX dyes@TiO2 complexes (Section 3.3) showed that the adsorption energies (ranging from
-
8.260 to
-
11.373 eV) are much greater than the binding energy of the GQD-ND1-I2 complex, indicating that the charge recombination process is unfavorable compared to the electron injection process, which is good for enhancing the photovoltaic performance of the GQD-ND1 nanocomposite in such applications. We can safely generalize this conclusion to other GQD-NDIX nanocomposites.

For the resultant complexes (NDIX-GQD-TiO2)/GQD-NDIX-TiO2/NDIX-TiO2, the adsorption energies (
E
a d s
) of all optimized complexes were calculated using the following equation:

a d s
= E
( N D I X - G Q D -
T i O
2
)

E
N D I X - G Q D

T i O
2

where
E ( N D I X - G Q D -
T i O
2
) is the energy term of the complex system due to the interaction between the NDIX-GQD nanocomposite and TiO2 cluster and
E
N D I X - G Q D
and
E
T i O
2
are the energy terms of the free components of the complex system. As adopted in many studies [6,10–13,72,73], to model GQD-NDIX nanocomposites, we attached the NDIX molecule to GQDs through amide linkage (amidation reaction). Furthermore, Li et al. [23], in their study of the adsorption process of an organic molecule DM on GQDs modeled with C54H18 through three adsorption modes, i.e., (i) amidation linkage, (ii) free radical addition, and (iii) cycloaddition reaction to theoretically investigate the best linkage configuration between the photosensitizer and GQD, found that to enhance the photoelectric conversion efficiency, the optical and electrical properties of DSSCs, the amide linkage performed the best. Additionally, to model the NDIX-GQD nanocomposite with a TiO2 complex, the NDIX-GQD-TiO2 complex, we attached the GQD molecule with a Ti atom in one of the (Ti2O4H)3 clusters through amide linkages [6].

To compare the adsorption energies of all possible adsorbed systems, the adsorption of NDIXs on TiO2 is simulated using three configurations: (i) the isolated ND adsorbed on TiO2 (ND-TiO2), (ii) the nanocomposite (GQD-ND) is adsorbed on TiO2 by the interaction of GQD with TiO2 (ND-GQD-TiO2), and (iii) the interaction of the nanocomposite occurs through ND (GQD-ND-TiO2). The optimized geometries of the twelve adsorbed systems at the C-PCM/B3LYP-D with 6-31G(d,p) and LANL2DZ basis sets in methanol solvent are depicted in Fig. 12 . The more stable complexes between the ND-GQD-TiO2 and GQD-ND-TiO2 complexes are GQD-ND-TiO2 complexes based on the energies of their optimized geometries, i.e., the values in parentheses in Fig. 12. The energies of the GQD-ND-TiO2 optimized complexes are more negative.

Table 6 and Fig. 13 show the adsorption energies of NDIX-TiO2, GQD-NDIX-TiO2, and NDIX- GQD-TiO2 complexes in methanol solvent at the C-PCM/B3LYP-D with 6-31G(d,p) and LANL2DZ basis sets. The adsorption energies (
E
a d s
), see Equation 3, of all adsorbed systems are negative and significantly large, suggesting spontaneous and chemisorption adsorption processes, respectively. The results demonstrated that the adsorption energies of ND-GQD-TiO2 complexes are more negative than those of GQD-ND-TiO2 complexes, indicating that the binding of the GQD-ND nanocomposites through the amide linkage formed between ND and TiO2 is preferable than that between GQD and TiO2. This conclusion is supported by results from the work of Teymourinia et al. [72]. The adsorption energies of the four adsorbed ND-GQD-TiO2 samples fall within a small range since the difference between the minimum and maximum values does not exceed 0.192 eV, while in the GQD-ND-TiO2 adsorbed systems, this difference is more significant and equals 0.713 eV. Among the four GQD-ND-TiO2 adsorbed systems, ND2-GQD-TiO2 shows the most negative adsorption energy (
E
a d s
= - 9.675
eV). However, the adsorption energies of the isolated dyes on TiO2 (ND-TiO2 complexes) are more negative, indicating that the nanocomposites may not induce better adsorption on the TiO2 semiconductor than the isolated NDs.

Fig. 14 represents the simulated UV–Vis absorption spectra of NDIX-TiO2, NDIX-GQD-TiO2, and GQD-NDIX-TiO2 adsorbed systems calculated at the C-PCM/CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) in methanol solvent. As seen from Fig. 7 and Table 3, the
λ
m a x
c a l c
values of isolated NDIX dyes are ND1 (348.81 nm), ND2 (479.48 nm), ND3 (490.89 nm), and ND4 (496.97 nm). Regarding these values for the adsorbed systems, NDIX-TiO2 is ND1-GQD (352 nm), ND2-GQD (348 nm), ND3-GQD (468 nm), and ND4-GQD (478 nm). On the other hand,
λ
m a x
c a l c
values for NDIX-GQD nanocomposites are: ND1-GQD (442 nm); ND2-GQD (489 nm); ND3-GQD (432 nm); ND4-GQD (432 nm), for the adsorbed systems NDIX-GQD-TiO2 they are: ND1-GQD-TiO2 (408 nm); ND2-GQD-TiO2 (408 nm); ND3-GQD-TiO2 (430 nm); ND4-GQD-TiO2 (536 nm), while for GQD-NDIX-TiO2 adsorbed systems they are: GQD-ND1-TiO2 (388 nm); GQD-ND2-TiO2 (388 nm); GQD-ND3-TiO2 (380 nm); GQD-ND4-TiO2 (380 nm). The hybridization between the natural dye and GQD results in a clear shift in the maximum absorption wavelength of the resultant nanocomposites due to the change in the electronic states of the two monomers (dye and GQD) and the extension of π-conjugation in the nanocomposite systems. In most cases, the
λ
m a x
c a l c
values of the adsorbed systems are blueshifted (shorter wavelengths) with respect to those of their isolated NDIX/NDIX-GQD nanocomposites. The adsorbed nanocomposites exhibit a significant intense and broad spectral response in the visible region compared to isolated systems or NDIX-TiO2 adsorbed systems, which is beneficial to enhance the efficiency of DSSCs. Since the intensities (oscillator strengths) of the maximum absorption bands of NDIX-GQD adsorbed systems are much lower than those of the other two adsorbed systems, i.e., GQD-NDIX-TiO2 and NDIX-GQD-TiO2, a result confirmed on the beneficial of hybrid the NDIX with GQD for efficient light harvesting property, see Fig. 14. In addition, the
λ
m a x
c a l c
values of adsorbed systems NDIX-GQD-TiO2 are redshifted with respect to those of GQD-NDIX-TiO2, which suggests that the adsorption on TiO2 through the NDIX unit is more beneficial than through the GQD unit, this aligns with the trend observed for the adsorption energies, Section 3.3.1.

As pointed out in Section 3.2.7 that the CAM-B3LYP functional is more capable of describing the NLO parameters, then the NLO parameters of the NDIX@TiO2 adsorbed systems were calculated at C-PCM/CAM-B3LYP with a 6-31G(d,p) basis set in methanol solvent and are listed in Table 7 . The

to

esu, those of the NDIX-GQD-TiO2 systems range from

esu to

esu, and those of the GQD-NDIX-TiO2 systems range from

10
- 29
esu to

10
- 29
esu. Therefore, the
β 0
values increase in the order NDIX-TiO2 < GQD-NDIX-TiO2 < NDIX-GQD-TiO2. We should note that the
β 0
values of the adsorbed systems are significantly larger than those of isolated NDIX (ranging from
4 ×

esu to

esu) and those of NDIX-GQD nanocomposites (ranging from

esu); see Table 5. On the hand, the

values of the NDIX-TiO2 systems range from

to

esu, those of the NDIX-GQD-TiO2 systems range from

esu. Therefore, the

values. This result implies that the adsorption of either the NDIX single dye/GQD-NDIX nanocomposites on TiO2 leads to elongation of the charge distance [74], thus better intramolecular charge transfer (IACT) and superior photovoltaic performance. Furthermore, the dipole moments of adsorbed systems on TiO2 are generally larger than those of isolated dyes/NDIX-GQD nanocomposites, indicating a better charge separation and thus better IACT process in the adsorbed systems (see Table 5 and Table 7).

The main conclusions derived from the DFT/TDDFT with B3LYP/CAM-B3LYP functionals and 6-31G(d,p)/LANL2DZ basis sets in C-PCM calculations of the GQD-ND nanocomposites and the adsorbed and binding systems on TiO2 clusters and with I2 molecules, respectively, are as follows:
GQD-ND nanocomposites have perfect matching of HOMO and LUMO energy levels to efficiently inject electrons into the CB of TiO2 and regenerate the sensitizer by the RP of the electrolyte system.
The energy gaps of the GQD-NDIX nanocomposites are efficiently tuned, and the charge excitation performance is improved.
The nanocomposites harvested visible light with much higher intensities than the isolated NDs.
The charge transfer performance and
J
sc
values for the nanocomposites are better than those of their isolated NDIX, and the ND2-GQD nanocomposite has the most efficient
EA
,
ω
,
ω
+
, and
h
values.
The larger values of
D
CT
,
q
CT
, and
μ
CT
and smaller values of
H
and
t
of the nanocomposites indicate that they can efficiently enhance the IACT properties.
The
μ
CT
,
β 0
, and
α 0
of the nanocomposite materials are greater/much greater than their components (NDs), indicating the better NLO properties of the nanocomposites. Therefore, these nanocomposites will show a higher
η
than isolated NDs.
Enhancement of the NLO properties of NDIX-TiO2, NDIX-GQD-TiO2, and GQD-NDIX-TiO2 adsorbed system compared to isolated dyes and GQD-NDIX nanocomposites, and thus elongation of charge separation and better IACT properties.
This theoretical work can be used as evidence of the efficiency of GQD-NDIX photosensitizers as visible light harvesters in DSSC applications.
Authors confirm that the data supporting this study's findings are available within the article and its supplementary data (SD).
